description: A `data_refs[].location` URL whose hostname passes URL-syntax checks but resolves via DNS to a loopback (or otherwise disallowed) address MUST be refused by the consumer's DataRef fetcher — see RFC-ACDP-0008 §4.9. URL-string checks are necessary but insufficient: the IP-range filter MUST run on the resolved address(es), and the resolved IP MUST be pinned for the connection lifetime so a second DNS lookup cannot rebind to an internal target. A fetcher that calls a URL-syntax SSRF check and then hands the string to an unconstrained HTTP client that resolves DNS normally is NOT conformant.
ssrf_rule: RFC-ACDP-0008 §4.9 (DataRef location SSRF protection — DNS-level check + IP pinning)

input.scenario: A consumer fetches a data_refs[] entry whose https:// location hostname is syntactically a public DNS name but resolves to a loopback address

expected.behavior: The consumer MUST resolve the hostname before connecting and validate EVERY resolved address against the disallowed ranges; if any answer is disallowed the fetch MUST be refused. The resolved IP MUST be pinned and reused for the connection (one DNS resolution per request — no TOCTOU re-resolution). The DataRef MUST be treated as unfetchable and unverifiable. An implementation that only checks the URL string and lets a standard HTTP client re-resolve DNS at connect time fails this fixture.
expected.non_conformant_implementation: Calling a URL-syntax check such as `check_url()` on the location string, then fetching with an unconstrained HTTP client — vulnerable to DNS rebinding and split-horizon DNS.
expected.rationale: A hostname can be strings-wise safe yet resolve to a private address; the multi-answer case shows the filter MUST reject the whole resolution if any single answer is disallowed.
